news_summary: Today’s positive news highlights the rediscovery of a lost cultural treasure by a French cinema pioneer and the simple yet effective approach to fostering genuine social connections.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles d'aujourd'hui mettent en lumière la redécouverte d'un trésor culturel perdu par un pionnier du cinéma français, ainsi qu'une approche simple mais efficace pour favoriser de véritables liens sociaux.
news_summary_es: Las buenas noticias de hoy destacan el redescubrimiento de un tesoro cultural perdido por parte de un pionero del cine francés y el enfoque sencillo, pero eficaz, para fomentar vínculos sociales auténticos.

articles.title: Lost film by French cinema pioneer Georges Méliès retrieved from US attic
articles.summary: "Gugusse and the Automaton", a short silent film made by French cinema pioneer Georges Méliès back in 1897, was lost to history until it was found in a trove of film reels donated to the US Library of Congress. The precious celluloid belonged to a potato farmer who showed silent movies to audiences in rural Pennsylvania at the turn of the 20th century.
articles.content:
A still from "Gugusse and the Automaton" by George Méliès, one of the fathers of the movie pictures.

The battered wooden trunk had been in the family for a century – shifted from attic to barn to garage as it was handed down through the generations. No one knew a cinematic treasure was inside.

That was until retired high school teacher Bill McFarland's curiosity got the better of him.

For the past 20 years, McFarland, 76, had been the keeper of the trunk, which originally belonged to his late great-grandfather who showed silent movies to audiences in rural Pennsylvania at the turn of the 20th century.

"It was just this trunk of films that seemed too good to throw away. But I had no idea what they were or how to show them," McFarland told AFP.

Bill McFarland holds up a photo of his great-grandfather, William DeLyle Frisbee, who left his family a cache of old silent movies © Jeff Kowalsky, AFP

He offered them to museums and even tried to sell them through an antique store, whose owner soon told him to take them away after learning vintage nitrate film reels were highly combustible and could explode.

Then last summer, McFarland drove from his home in the northern state of Michigan to the US Library of Congress’ National Audio-Visual Conservation Center in Culpeper in the southern state of Virginia.

He was in for a surprise – a pleasant one.

Read moreCannes reclaims Napoleon for France with ‘rebirth’ of Abel Gance epic

Pioneering short film

Spliced in the middle of one of the 10 reels was a lost short film by Georges Méliès, a French cinema pioneer – the first to experiment with fictional narratives and special effects at the very dawn of moving pictures.

The 45-second film was made in 1897 – just two years after the world's first public screening of a movie in Paris © Kent Nishimura, AFP

The 45-second film, "Gugusse and the Automaton," was made in 1897 – just two years after the Lumière Brothers staged the world's first public screening of a movie in Paris.

Méliès, a theatrical showman and magician, attended that screening and was inspired to make films of his own. He is most famous for "A Trip to the Moon" (1902) with its iconic scene of a rocket landing in the eye of the man in the Moon.

By a decade later, his filmmaking had fallen out of vogue as the center of the movie world shifted from Europe to America.

Méliès ended up as a toy seller in Paris’ Gare Montparnasse train station – a story that was dramatized in Martin Scorsese’s 2011 film, "Hugo". But his legacy endured.

Nitrate Film Vault technician Courtney Holschuh (L) and vault leader George Willeman (R) unspool film on reels at the Library of Congress Audio-Visual Conservation Center © Kent Nishimura, AFP

"He was one of the first filmmakers," said George Willeman, leader of the congressional library's nitrate film vault, who said the recovered reel was likely a third-generation copy of the Melies original. "And one of the first to experience film piracy."

Copy's miraculous survival

In retrospect, piracy was a salvation for film historians as it means that Méliès' work lives on.

Reputedly, he destroyed hundreds of his own negatives, and the celluloid was melted down – and some of it used as raw material to make soldiers' boots during World War I.

A deteriorated portion of the film reel of "Gugusse and the Automaton" © Kent Nishimura, AFP

While "Gugusse and the Automaton" was known to be in Melies' back catalogue, no one had seen it until McFarland delivered it to the library in his Toyota sedan last September.

It features a magician – played by Méliès – cranking up an automaton that grows in size and then beats the magician on the head with a stick. The magician retaliates by bashing the automaton with a sledgehammer until it disappears, shrinking through a surprisingly slick series of jump cuts.

The recovered reel is likely a third-generation copy of the Georges Melies' original © Kent Nishimura, AFP

"These single frame cuts are really precise for a movie this old, and the gags are timeless," said Jason Evans Groth, curator of the library's moving image section, who recounted McFarland popping the trunk of his car with the film reels inside when he arrived in Culpeper.

The film's discovery has taken McFarland on another journey – learning about the life of his great-grandfather William DeLyle Frisbee.

'Ticking time bomb'

Born in 1860 in the rural northwest of Pennsylvania, Frisbee was a stocky, mustached man with many strings to his bow.

The film's discovery has taken Bill McFarland on another journey -- learning about the life of his great-grandfather William DeLyle Frisbee © The McFarland Family, AFP

He grew potatoes, kept bees, made maple syrup and taught school three months each year. In his downtime he would travel by horse and buggy across Pennsylvania and neighboring states with what he called his "exhibition": a new-fangled Edison phonograph, a magic lantern slide projector and later on, movies.

Well-thumbed pocket diaries describe Frisbee's travels. "Gave the exhibition at Garland, $5 receipts, rough crowd," reads one entry, referring to a community in northwestern Pennsylvania.

"I can only imagine Saturday night, they might have been liquored up a little bit," observed McFarland. "I wonder if there were disappointed customers, or if they were just rowdy? Maybe they were excited at seeing these pictures."

Read moreThe Passion of Carl Dreyer: Paris celebrates cinema’s ‘forgotten’ master

A century on, and the archivists at the Library of Congress were excited too.

An alarmed McFarland watched specialists whisk the precious reels to a refrigerated vault, already home to tens of thousands of films from the golden age of Hollywood – and specially designed to prevent a nitrate-fueled fire.

Library film preservation specialists spent a week restoring the film reel frame-by-frame and digitizing it © Kent Nishimura, AFP

"It finally really registered that I had been...carrying a ticking time bomb," McFarland said.

Library film preservation specialists spent a week restoring the film reel frame-by-frame and digitizing it. The reel was shrunken through age and frayed, but otherwise in remarkable condition for something stashed in sun-heated attics for years.

It’s now a piece of cinema history, viewable on the library’s website.

What is 'The Bright Side'? For those tired of anxiety-inducing news, The Bright Side highlights initiatives, new discoveries and lighter perspectives to showcase progress and positivity in the world. Whether covering scientific innovations, inspiring profiles or striking images, our stories are always based on rigorously verified facts.

(FRANCE 24 with AFP)
articles.source: News and stories on CULTURE, movies, music, literature - France 24
articles.reasoning: The discovery and preservation of a lost film by cinema pioneer Georges Méliès represents a significant cultural and historical achievement with broad public interest. It preserves an important piece of early film history, enriches cultural heritage, and showcases successful archival restoration efforts. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights the positive impact of preserving artistic legacy for future generations.
articles.personality_title: Lost 1897 film by Georges Méliès found in US attic and restored
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Georges Méliès was one of the first filmmakers and special effects pioneers in the late 1800s. Many of his early films were lost or destroyed over time, making any discovery of his work important for movie history.

**What happened** – A 45-second silent film called "Gugusse and the Automaton," made by Méliès in 1897, was found inside a trunk of old film reels in rural Pennsylvania. The reels belonged to William DeLyle Frisbee, a potato farmer and silent movie exhibitor from the early 1900s. His great-grandson, Bill McFarland, brought the films to the US Library of Congress last year, where experts identified and restored the lost Méliès film.

**Impact** – This film is a rare surviving example of Méliès’ early work, showing his creative use of special effects and storytelling just two years after the first public movies were shown. It adds to our understanding of cinema’s beginnings and preserves a piece of cultural history that might have been lost forever. The discovery also sheds light on how silent movies were shared in small American towns long ago.

**What's next step** – The Library of Congress has digitized the film so people can watch it online. Archivists will continue to preserve the other reels from Frisbee’s collection. This find may inspire more searches for lost films in private collections and attics, helping to uncover forgotten parts of film history.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A lost film by early cinema pioneer Georges Méliès was discovered in an American attic, restored, and shared with the public, enriching the history of movies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un film perdu de 1897 de Georges Méliès retrouvé dans un grenier américain et restauré
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Georges Méliès fut l’un des premiers cinéastes et pionniers des effets spéciaux à la fin des années 1800. Beaucoup de ses films anciens ont été perdus ou détruits, rendant chaque découverte importante pour l’histoire du cinéma.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un court film muet de 45 secondes intitulé « Gugusse et l’automate », réalisé par Méliès en 1897, a été retrouvé dans un coffre contenant des bobines de films anciennes en Pennsylvanie rurale. Ces bobines appartenaient à William DeLyle Frisbee, un cultivateur de pommes de terre et exhibiteur de films muets au début des années 1900. Son arrière-petit-fils, Bill McFarland, a apporté les films à la Bibliothèque du Congrès des États-Unis l’année dernière, où des experts ont identifié et restauré ce film perdu de Méliès.

**Impact** – Ce film est un rare exemple survivant du travail précoce de Méliès, montrant son utilisation créative d’effets spéciaux et de narration seulement deux ans après les premières projections publiques. Il enrichit notre compréhension des débuts du cinéma et préserve un morceau d’histoire culturelle qui aurait pu être perdu à jamais. La découverte éclaire aussi la manière dont les films muets étaient diffusés dans les petites villes américaines.

**Prochaine étape** – La Bibliothèque du Congrès a numérisé le film pour permettre au public de le visionner en ligne. Les archivistes continueront de préserver les autres bobines de la collection de Frisbee. Cette découverte pourrait encourager d’autres recherches de films perdus dans des collections privées et greniers, aidant à révéler des pans oubliés de l’histoire du cinéma.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un film perdu du pionnier du cinéma Georges Méliès a été retrouvé dans un grenier américain, restauré et partagé avec le public, enrichissant l’histoire du cinéma.
articles.personality_title_es: Película perdida de 1897 de Georges Méliès encontrada en un ático de EE.UU. y restaurada
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Georges Méliès fue uno de los primeros cineastas y pioneros en efectos especiales a finales del siglo XIX. Muchas de sus primeras películas se perdieron o destruyeron, por lo que cada hallazgo es importante para la historia del cine.

**Qué pasó** – Se encontró una película muda de 45 segundos llamada "Gugusse y el autómata", realizada por Méliès en 1897, dentro de un baúl con rollos de película en una zona rural de Pensilvania. Los rollos pertenecían a William DeLyle Frisbee, un agricultor de papas y exhibidor de películas mudas a principios del siglo XX. Su bisnieto, Bill McFarland, llevó las películas a la Biblioteca del Congreso de EE.UU. el año pasado, donde expertos identificaron y restauraron la película perdida de Méliès.

**Impacto** – Esta película es un raro ejemplo que sobrevive del trabajo temprano de Méliès, mostrando su uso creativo de efectos especiales y narrativa apenas dos años después de las primeras proyecciones públicas. Añade a nuestra comprensión de los inicios del cine y preserva una parte de la historia cultural que pudo haberse perdido para siempre. El hallazgo también muestra cómo se compartían las películas mudas en pequeños pueblos estadounidenses.

**Próximo paso** – La Biblioteca del Congreso digitalizó la película para que la gente pueda verla en línea. Los archivistas seguirán preservando los otros rollos de la colección de Frisbee. Este descubrimiento puede inspirar más búsquedas de películas perdidas en colecciones privadas y áticos, ayudando a recuperar partes olvidadas de la historia del cine.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una película perdida del pionero del cine Georges Méliès fue encontrada en un ático estadounidense, restaurada y compartida con el público, enriqueciendo la historia del cine.
articles.image_prompt: An old, weathered wooden trunk partially open in a softly lit attic, with delicate vintage film reels glowing gently inside; beside it, a nostalgic antique magic lantern projector and a small automaton figure mid-transformation, all rendered in warm, muted earth tones with textured brushstrokes evoking a sense of rediscovered cinematic history and timeless wonder.

articles.title: The low-effort way to build real connection: finding your third place
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Most people have two main places: home and work. The idea that you need a third sounds obvious the moment you hear it, which is maybe why sociologist Ray Oldenburg felt the need to write a whole book about it in 1989. That book, The Great Good Place, named […]
The post The low-effort way to build real connection: finding your third place first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Most people have two main places: home and work. The idea that you need a third sounds obvious the moment you hear it, which is maybe why sociologist Ray Oldenburg felt the need to write a whole book about it in 1989.

That book, The Great Good Place, named the concept: “third places” are spaces where people gather outside home and work, with no particular agenda, just to be somewhere together. The village square, the barbershop, the front stoop. The coffee shop where the same people show up every Tuesday and eventually learn each other’s names.

“Third places are the environments where belonging has room to take shape,” says Spud Marshall, author of Designing Creative Communities.

The trouble is that these spaces have been designed out of the way most Americans live.

How modern life pushed them out

Lucy Rose, founder of the Cost of Loneliness Project, traces the decline to overlapping forces. “Zoning laws often divide residential areas from shops, cafés, and gathering spaces, which means people must drive long distances to reach everyday meeting spots,” she says. “Car-dependent communities keep people moving quickly from place to place rather than lingering in shared spaces. Remote work has also reduced the number of casual interactions that once happened during the workday. As a result, many people now move between two primary environments: home and screens.”

Without a third place, the only way to connect with other people is to schedule it, perform it, or scroll toward a digital approximation of it.

Third places work because they ask so little. “They allow people to arrive and simply be present,” Rose says. “You do not need an invitation or a plan for conversation. You only need to show up.” That low bar is the whole point. These spaces give people a chance “to exchange ideas, build relationships, and feel like they have a role in shaping the place where they live,” Marshall says.

Ten places worth trying

The best third place is usually the one that already fits your habits.

Coffee shops

Cafés invite lingering by design. “People come at similar times each day and begin recognizing one another. Familiarity builds quickly in spaces like this,” Rose says. Coffee shops draw a mix of freelancers, students, and remote workers who want somewhere to be outside the house without needing a particular reason, which is exactly the kind of low-key regularity that turns strangers into familiar faces.

Parks and community gardens

These lush spaces offer connection without requiring it. Rene Mondy, a licensed professional counselor and founder of the Solo Dining Directory, notes that walking paths and benches let you choose your level of engagement on any given day. If you want something more social, volunteer at a community garden. “Working side-by-side creates conversation without forcing it,” Rose says. “People tend to return through the growing season, which gives relationships time to develop naturally.”

Maker’s spaces

Think: pottery studios, woodshops, fabrication labs. These creative hubs attract people who would rather build something together than make small talk. “These spaces tend to attract builders, artists, tinkerers, and curious learners,” Marshall says. “Conversation tends to emerge naturally, and collaborations often follow.”

Restaurants

“They offer many of my counseling clients structure, such as seating, ordering, and sensory engagement through food and conversation,” says Mondy. Becoming a regular somewhere is an easy way to be known without having to introduce yourself each time.

Farmer’s markets

Farmer’s markets have a built-in weekly rhythm that makes casual encounters almost inevitable. “The routine of a Saturday market becomes a shared community rhythm where casual interactions happen easily,” Rose says. These events, Marshall adds, create “a low-pressure environment where families, neighbors, and local entrepreneurs all interact in the same space.”

Public libraries

A library could be the most accessible third place in most towns: free and open to everyone. “They serve students, parents with young kids, job seekers, retirees, and lifelong learners,” Marshall says. Many also offer book clubs, language classes, and local programming that give people a reason to return. Libraries are closing across the country due to funding cuts, so showing up has benefits in both directions.

Museums

Museums suit anyone who wants to be around people without the obligation to talk. “Museums and galleries naturally support reflection,” Mondy says. “Ultimately, third places give us permission to pause, which allows us to grow.”

Dog parks

Dog parks remove the awkwardness of introducing yourself to a stranger. “Dogs often make introductions easier than people do,” Rose says. “Owners return with predictable routines and begin seeing the same faces again and again.”

Gyms and fitness studios

Spaces built around classes or groups create the kind of repeated contact that gradually becomes familiarity. “Running clubs, pickleball groups, and yoga classes bring people together around a shared activity. Repeated participation builds familiarity and trust over time,” Rose says.

Bookstores

Bookshops, particularly independent ones, are worth seeking out partly for their own sake. Like libraries, they’re closing faster than they’re opening. “Bookstores encourage curiosity and quiet exploration,” Mondy says. “They allow people to browse and spend time without needing to interact unless they want to.”

The variety matters because different things work for different people and different moods. A good third place doesn’t require you to be socially ready, only present. Most are closer to your daily life than you’d expect. The trick is stopping instead of passing through.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the importance of 'third places'—social environments outside home and work that foster community connection and belonging. It discusses the societal decline of such spaces due to urban design and remote work, and offers practical examples of accessible third places that can improve social well-being and reduce loneliness on a broad scale. The story is focused, detailed, and promotes a positive social solution with meaningful impact on community mental health and cohesion.
articles.personality_title: Finding Your Third Place: A Simple Way to Build Real Connections
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Most people spend their time between two main places: home and work. Sociologist Ray Oldenburg introduced the idea of a “third place” in 1989, meaning a social space outside of home and work where people can casually gather and connect.

**What happened** – The article explains that these third places, like coffee shops, parks, and libraries, have become less common in many communities due to city planning, car dependence, and the rise of remote work. Without these spaces, people often miss out on casual social interactions and feel more isolated. Experts suggest that finding or creating a third place can help people connect naturally without pressure.

**Impact** – Third places offer a low-effort way to build friendships and a sense of belonging. They don’t require plans or invitations—just showing up is enough. Examples include coffee shops where regular visitors recognize each other, community gardens where people work alongside one another, and dog parks where dogs help break the ice. These spaces help reduce loneliness and create stronger communities by encouraging repeated, casual contact.

**What's next step** – People can look for local third places that fit their interests and habits, such as farmer’s markets, gyms, or bookstores, and make an effort to spend time there regularly. Communities might also consider designing more shared spaces that invite casual gathering. Supporting places like libraries and independent bookstores can also help keep these important third places alive.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Spending time in a welcoming third place outside home and work can help people build real connections with less effort and more natural interaction.
articles.personality_title_fr: Trouver son troisième lieu : une façon simple de créer de vraies connexions
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La plupart des gens passent leur temps entre deux lieux principaux : la maison et le travail. En 1989, le sociologue Ray Oldenburg a présenté l’idée du « troisième lieu », un espace social en dehors de la maison et du travail où les gens peuvent se retrouver et se connecter sans pression.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – L’article explique que ces troisièmes lieux, comme les cafés, les parcs ou les bibliothèques, ont disparu dans beaucoup de communautés à cause de l’urbanisme, de la dépendance à la voiture et du télétravail. Sans ces espaces, les échanges sociaux spontanés se font rares, ce qui peut renforcer le sentiment d’isolement. Des experts recommandent de trouver ou de créer un troisième lieu pour se connecter naturellement.

**Impact** – Les troisièmes lieux offrent un moyen simple de créer des amitiés et un sentiment d’appartenance. Ils ne demandent pas de plan ni d’invitation – il suffit juste d’y aller. Par exemple, les cafés où les habitués se reconnaissent, les jardins communautaires où l’on travaille côte à côte, ou les parcs à chiens où les animaux facilitent les rencontres. Ces espaces aident à réduire la solitude et à renforcer la communauté.

**Prochaine étape** – Chacun peut chercher un troisième lieu local qui correspond à ses goûts, comme un marché, une salle de sport ou une librairie, et essayer d’y aller régulièrement. Les communautés peuvent aussi créer plus d’espaces partagés qui invitent à se rassembler. Soutenir les bibliothèques et les librairies indépendantes aide à préserver ces lieux importants.

**Une phrase clé** – Passer du temps dans un troisième lieu accueillant, en dehors de la maison et du travail, aide à créer de vraies connexions par des interactions naturelles et sans effort.
articles.personality_title_es: Encontrar tu tercer lugar: una forma sencilla de crear conexiones reales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La mayoría de las personas divide su tiempo entre dos lugares principales: el hogar y el trabajo. En 1989, el sociólogo Ray Oldenburg presentó la idea del “tercer lugar”, un espacio social fuera de casa y el trabajo donde la gente puede reunirse y conectarse sin presiones.

**Qué pasó** – El artículo explica que estos terceros lugares, como cafeterías, parques y bibliotecas, han desaparecido en muchas comunidades debido al diseño urbano, la dependencia del automóvil y el trabajo remoto. Sin estos espacios, las interacciones sociales casuales son menos frecuentes, lo que puede aumentar el aislamiento. Expertos sugieren encontrar o crear un tercer lugar para conectar de forma natural.

**Impacto** – Los terceros lugares ofrecen una forma fácil de crear amistades y sentido de pertenencia. No requieren planes ni invitaciones, solo presentarse. Ejemplos incluyen cafeterías donde los visitantes habituales se reconocen, jardines comunitarios donde se trabaja juntos, y parques para perros donde las mascotas facilitan las presentaciones. Estos espacios ayudan a reducir la soledad y a fortalecer las comunidades.

**Próximo paso** – Las personas pueden buscar un tercer lugar local que se adapte a sus intereses, como mercados, gimnasios o librerías, y tratar de visitarlo regularmente. Las comunidades también pueden diseñar más espacios compartidos que inviten a reunirse. Apoyar bibliotecas y librerías independientes ayuda a mantener estos lugares importantes.

**Una frase clave** – Pasar tiempo en un tercer lugar acogedor fuera del hogar y el trabajo ayuda a crear conexiones reales con menos esfuerzo y más interacción natural.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy, softly lit coffee shop interior with empty mismatched chairs and tables arranged in a welcoming circle, a steaming cup of coffee at the center, surrounded by subtle outlines of diverse everyday objects from parks, libraries, and maker’s tools gently blending into the background, all rendered in warm, muted earth tones and gentle brushstrokes evoking a calm, inviting atmosphere of shared presence and casual connection.
